title: New Beginning
html:
"Everything's all set up," Lucas muttered, stepping back to look at the table. The strawberry grape cake sat in the center with one candle lit, the birthday song playing from his phone, a small dish he'd set beside it for the cake slice he was about to cut. There was nobody else in the room to appreciate any of this, which he was aware of.

He was 16 today. His skin was darker than most people's in the village, tanned from construction work and daily fishing, and around here that read as a shortage of money. It was. He'd been working both jobs since he was old enough for someone to hire him, which had started younger than it probably should have.

His parents had died at the fish market when he was 10. A truck lost its brakes at the worst possible moment and that was that, both of them gone in the same afternoon. Nobody in the area had taken him in afterward, and he hadn't blamed them for it. The poverty in the village was thorough enough that adding a child to a household was a real cost, and a 10-year-old with no useful skills yet was worse than useless to most of them. He'd gotten by doing errands and household chores for neighbors, earning small amounts and the occasional meal, keeping his head down. Six years later he was still managing fine.

Their faces had started blurring at the edges lately, specific details going soft the way they do. He noticed it the way he'd notice a fraying rope, something to register and move past.

'Wish they were here,' he thought, looking at the cake.

He let that pass and leaned toward the candle. "Mom, Dad, your son Lucas is 16 today," he murmured, eyes moving around the empty room. He sighed once, blew out the candle, and cut himself a slice.

The crackle of static hit his ear a few minutes later.

"Zzzzz...z...zz."

Lucas paused, fork still in hand. "Hmm? What's that?" He looked around the room, which was empty, same as it had been, his nearest neighbor 12 feet away. He sat still, and then the static sharpened into something unmistakable.

---
"HUMAN OF EARTH! INITIATING EARTH MUTATION PROGRAM PHASE 1!"
---

"Huh? Phase one of what?" he muttered, turning the words over. The sensible explanation was auditory hallucinations, and he had no better candidate, so he went with that, finished his slice of cake, and went to bed.

'Yummy,' had been his last coherent thought. For what it was worth.

Sometime after he'd fallen asleep, a scream tore through the neighborhood from somewhere outside. Lucas pulled his blanket over his head. "Who's making that racket?" he grumbled into the pillow. "Some people have no consideration."

***

January 21, 2029

"Last night's sleep was incredibly refreshing," Lucas remarked to himself as he stretched. It was Sunday, nothing on the schedule, which put him in a good mood without much effort. He lay there an extra moment taking stock of the quiet, then got up and headed outside to catch something for breakfast, stepping through the front door to find his boat gone from the dock.

He stood at the water's edge and looked across the lake. The boat wasn't visible anywhere on the surface, not close in and not further out. It was old and beat-up enough that stealing it made almost no practical sense, and yet it was gone. "And I was hoping the first day of 16 would start better than this," he muttered, letting out a disheartened sigh.

Something near where the boat usually sat caught his attention, a fish about 30 cm long, holding still just below the surface. Lucas had been fishing long enough to name most local species without thinking. This one he couldn't place. The proportions were slightly off, the fin placement and body depth not matching anything he could pull from memory. He watched it for a second and went for his spear.

"Swoosh!" The strike hit clean and the fish went still, green blood spreading out through the water around it.

Lucas pulled it out and turned it over, the blood genuinely green rather than any trick of the light, and examined the wound. "Is this even safe to eat?"

He cleaned it anyway. The flesh looked normal enough once you got past the outer strangeness, and he fried it up, sat down at the table with chopsticks and a small dish of soy sauce, held a piece up for inspection, and put it in his mouth.

"Wow, this tastes even better than I expected," he exclaimed, sitting back slightly. As a fisherman he'd eaten a lot of fish in a lot of conditions. This ranked near the top by any measure, which was saying something given the green blood situation.

The static came back while he was still chewing, sharper than the night before.

---
[Mutated Fish Species Detected, Commencing System Profile!]

[Opening System!]
---

"System?" Lucas said, and a large holographic screen materialized in the air in front of him.

---
Name: Lucas Tharla

Age: 16 years old

Race: Human

Gender: Male

Class: None

Subclass: None

Subclass: None

Subclass: None

Level: 0 (0/10)

Stats:

Agi: 10 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Str: 20 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Int: 25 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Vit: 25 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Unallocated Stats Point: 0

Skills:

Unallocated Skills Point: 0
---

He read through it. Stats and levels were things from the games on his phone, not from anything real, and a large floating holographic screen was very much not something that happened to people eating breakfast. He sat with that for a moment, then thought back to the notification: mutated fish. The system had activated right after he ate it, which was either a coincidence or the whole point.

"Could this be linked to those mutated fish?" he pondered aloud, and turned to look at the lake.

About ten more of the same type were grouped in the shallows and he watched them for a few minutes. They started going for each other with more focused aggression than fish usually show, the smaller ones clearing out early, the group whittling down until two were left. Those two went at each other hard. When one stopped moving, the survivor ate it without hesitation and visibly grew, sitting now at about 40 cm where it had been noticeably smaller. Lucas watched all of this from where he stood, not moving.

The fish that had stopped moving was the one he'd already speared from earlier that morning, the wound still slowing it down. He picked up his spear and moved to the water's edge, lining up the shot on the larger fish while it was still close to shore.

"Slash!" The strike went in clean and he hauled the 40 cm fish out.

---
[Level 2 Mutated Fish Killed, Awarding 10 Exp!]

[Leveling Exp Reach: 10/10!]

[Leveling Up: Level 1!]

[Receiving 2 Stats Points and 1 Skills Point!]
---

"I can level up?" Lucas mused, looking at the fish with a perplexed expression. "This is so unscientific."

He said "System" and the screen opened.

---
[Opening System!]

Name: Lucas Tharla

Age: 16 years old

Race: Human

Gender: Male

Class: None

Subclass: None

Subclass: None

Subclass: None

Level: 1 (0/50)

Stats:

Agi: 10 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Str: 20 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Int: 25 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Vit: 25 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Unallocated Stats Point: 2

Skills:

Unallocated Skills Point: 1
---

"If leveling is real, skills probably are too. But there's nothing here," he mused thoughtfully, scanning the empty Skills section. A point to spend and nowhere to spend it yet. He closed the screen, cleaned the second fish, and fried it the same as the first, though the 40 cm size made it twice the work. A catch like that wasn't an everyday occurrence. He ate all of it over the next few hours and by the time he was done there was nothing left but bones.

His rice supply was nearly out. He needed the market, which meant leaving through the back door since the boat was gone, the back being the only exit with a connection to dry ground. He took the path he always used toward town.

Ten minutes in, he stopped. The road ahead was completely empty, which was unusual enough on a Sunday morning that he stood there for a moment trying to account for it. There was always someone on this road at this hour. He called out once. His voice carried down toward the tree line and came back off the fence posts with nothing behind it. The nearest house had its shutters open and nobody in the window, the cooking fire out, the yard to the side of it undisturbed. He had passed that house every day for years and it had never looked like that.

Lucas looked back the way he'd come, then forward again. The road looked exactly as it always had, which somehow made it worse.

"Huh...?" he muttered, brow furrowing.
